meta.title: Auth Overlap
meta.subject: HMAC vs signed JWT vs API key — what only the intersection earns

scenes.title: Auth Overlap
scenes.tagline: Three credentials. Three properties. One intersection earns the system.
scenes.footnote: HMAC  ·  signed JWT  ·  bearer API key

beats.narration: Three credentials show up over and over in service authentication. HMAC, signed JSON Web Tokens, and plain bearer API keys.
They look interchangeable in the docs -- a string in a header, a check on the server. They are not. The set of guarantees each one delivers is different, and the difference is the entire argument.
We will not rank them. We will show what only one specific intersection of properties earns -- and what the others quietly leave on the table.

scenes.heading: Three properties the credential might prove

nodes.label: Authenticated
nodes.sub: the server can verify the caller holds a real secret

nodes.label: Per-request integrity
nodes.sub: the request body cannot be tampered without detection

nodes.label: Replay-protected
nodes.sub: an attacker who captures the wire bytes cannot re-send them later

beats.narration: A credential can prove the caller knows a secret -- authentication. That is the obvious one, the only one most people check for.
It can also prove that the request body has not been tampered with -- per-request integrity, signed alongside the body, not just the header.
And it can prove the wire bytes are fresh -- replay-protected, so an attacker who sniffs traffic cannot replay yesterday's call today. Three properties. Not every scheme carries all three.

regions.label: API key
regions.note: proves possession of a secret, nothing more

regions.label: raw signature
regions.note: signs the body but no clock

novelty.claim: HMAC over body plus timestamp plus nonce is the only construction where the same bytes prove identity, prove the body, and expire in one verify step.

beats.narration: Put the three properties on a diagram. The plain API key sits in the leftmost slice -- it proves you hold the secret, and it proves nothing else. The body could be rewritten in flight; the bytes could be replayed an hour later. The key does not notice.

A signed JWT pushes one circle further. The short expiration adds replay protection. But the JWT signs its own payload, not the request body -- the body of the call still rides naked underneath. Authenticated and replay-protected, integrity still missing.

A raw signature over the body picks up integrity instead -- the body cannot drift -- but without a clock or a nonce, the same signed request is replayable forever. Two properties, two different missing thirds.

Now look at where all three circles overlap. HMAC over the body plus a timestamp plus a nonce is the only construction where one MAC verify proves identity, proves the body, and proves freshness -- a single check that closes all three properties at once. That is what the intersection earns.

nodes.label: Clock skew bound
nodes.sub: needs a synced wall clock, both sides

nodes.label: Nonce store
nodes.sub: the server must remember every nonce until it expires

nodes.label: Bearer key alone
nodes.sub: smaller and cacheable -- and silent on integrity, replay

beats.narration: The triple intersection is not free. A bearer API key is one cached lookup -- no clock, no state. We rejected that simpler path in favor of HMAC, and chose the triple over the single at the cost of two new failure modes.

First, the clock. The standard timestamp window is 300 seconds -- five minutes -- and any deployment with skew greater than that starts rejecting valid traffic. The trade-off is real: replay protection pays for itself with a synced wall clock as a hard dependency.

Second, the nonce store. The server has to remember every nonce for the full window -- 5 minutes of state per accepted request -- or the same signed request can be replayed once inside that window. A property the diagram promises only holds if the server pays the storage bill.

scenes.heading: The single line to carry away
scenes.statement: Pick the credential for the intersection of properties you actually need, not for the strongest property alone.

beats.narration: The lesson is set-theoretic, not nominal. Pick the credential for the intersection of properties you actually need -- authentication, integrity, replay protection -- not for the strongest single property on offer.

scenes.heading: Auth overlap — what to carry
points: Three properties: authenticated, per-request integrity, replay-protected.
API key carries one. Signed JWT carries two. Raw signature carries a different two.
Only HMAC with a timestamp and nonce closes the triple intersection.
Caveat — the triple costs a synced clock and a nonce store.
Carry: name the properties you need, then read the diagram, then choose.

beats.narration: The honest verdict. Three properties matter, and most credential choices are made by name -- pick a JWT, pick an API key -- not by which set of properties the scheme actually earns.
An API key proves identity and nothing else. A signed JWT adds replay protection but leaves the body unsigned. A raw signature picks up integrity but is replayable forever.
Only the HMAC construction with a timestamp and nonce sits at the triple intersection -- one verify proves all three.
The cost is real -- a synchronized clock and a nonce store. The triple is not free; it is paid for in coordination and storage.

So the durable move is the inversion. Name the set of properties the system genuinely needs, then read the diagram, then pick the scheme that sits in that intersection. The credential is a region, not a name.
